content_markdown: An ideal gas has a volume of $15\text{ ml}$, a temperature of $20\text{ }^\circ\text{C}$ and a pressure of $100\text{ kPa}$. The volume of the gas is reduced to $5\text{ ml}$ and the temperature is raised to $40\text{ }^\circ\text{C}$. What is the new pressure of the gas?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

vibe_explanation: To solve this, use the combined gas law $\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$. Remember that temperature must be in Kelvin ($T\text{ (K)} = T\text{ (}^\circ\text{C)} + 273$). The volume decreases by a factor of 3 (from 15 to 5), which would triple the pressure if temperature were constant. The temperature increases from $293\text{ K}$ to $313\text{ K}$, which is a small fractional increase. Therefore, the pressure should be slightly more than $300\text{ kPa}$ ($100 \times 3 \times \frac{313}{293} \approx 320\text{ kPa}$).
